metadata.summary: Organize content by user tasks and goals. Produces category structure, taxonomy, sitemap. Validate with card sorting and tree testing.

content.title: Information Architecture
content.summary: Organize content by user tasks and goals. Produces category structure, taxonomy, sitemap. Validate with card sorting and tree testing.

why.p: Navigation organized around internal product logic or org charts fails users who search by task or topic. A structured IA process (research → organize → validate) prevents the most common failure: building navigation that makes sense to the team but not to the user. 4-7 top-level categories is the validated sweet spot for scannability.

list.item: Generating a sitemap or taxonomy from a product spec, feature list, or content inventory
Auditing an existing IA against card sort or tree test data to identify mismatches
Drafting an IA strategy document from user research findings and business requirements
Proposing navigation label alternatives when current labels test poorly with users
Validating a proposed IA — agents draft structure but cannot replace card sorting and tree testing with real users
Real-time search relevance tuning — IA shapes browse; search ranking is a separate discipline
Micro-level layout decisions — IA governs content organization, not component placement within a page

rule.statement: Organize content around user tasks and goals, not internal product structure or org chart.
rule.rationale: Internal organization reflects how the team thinks, not how users search. Task-based IA (Shop, Learn, Get Help) maps to user intent; feature-based IA (Products, Platform, Resources) maps to company structure and fails navigation findability tests.

list.item: Topic-based: organize by subject matter (most common for content-heavy sites)
Task-based: organize by what users want to do (best for tool/application navigation)
Audience-based: organize by user type (use only when audiences have truly distinct needs)
Chronological: organize by time (logs, news, events)

rule.statement: Target 4-7 top-level categories; keep navigation to 3 levels maximum depth.
rule.rationale: Below 4 categories feels sparse and forces overloaded categories. Above 7 exceeds comfortable visual scanning range (Miller's Law). Deeper than 3 levels buries content in paths users cannot remember or navigate back from.

rule.statement: Write labels in user vocabulary; never use internal codenames, acronyms, or "Misc" / "Other" / "Resources" without specific scope.
rule.rationale: User-language labels require no translation. Internal terminology forces users to learn your vocabulary before they can navigate. "Resources" with no scope defined is the most common navigation label failure — it can contain anything and therefore signals nothing.

rule.statement: Validate labels with 5 users before running a tree test — a wrong label makes tree test data uninterpretable.
rule.rationale: If a label is misunderstood, users take wrong paths not because the structure is bad but because the label failed. Label validation is cheaper than rerunning a full tree test.

rule.statement: Define rules for where new content types will go before launch; do not add categories reactively when content accumulates.
rule.rationale: Reactive category creation produces inconsistent depth and "miscellaneous" buckets. Pre-defined taxonomy rules keep the IA coherent as content grows.

rule.statement: Use mega menus for sites with 40+ pages organized into 4+ top-level categories with meaningful subcategories at L2.
rule.rationale: Mega menus expose L2 content without requiring navigation into L1 first, reducing clicks for users who already know their destination category. Overuse on small sites creates visual complexity without benefit.

example.title: Mega menu for e-commerce site
example.note: E-commerce with Products, Solutions, Resources, Company: each top-level item expands to show 3-6 subcategories in columns, with a featured content block. Users can jump directly to "Enterprise Solutions" without clicking "Solutions" first.

rule.statement: Use hub-and-spoke for task-focused applications where users complete one task per session, then return to a central dashboard.
rule.rationale: Hub-and-spoke minimizes navigation complexity by removing the need for cross-spoke navigation. Users go from hub to task and back — no need to find "related" content or browse.

rule.statement: Use flat structure (1-2 levels) for small sites with under 20 pages, marketing sites, and portfolio sites.
rule.rationale: Small sites with deep hierarchies bury content unnecessarily. Flat structure keeps all content within 2 clicks of home, reducing navigation overhead for simple content sets.

rule.statement: Follow this mandatory validation sequence: card sort → proposed IA → label validation (5 users) → tree test → refined IA → build.
rule.rationale: Agent-generated or team-designed IA without validation produces navigation that makes sense to the team but fails with real users. Each validation step has a different failure mode; all three are required.

list.item: Card sort: discovers how users group content (inputs IA structure)
Label validation: confirms users interpret category labels as intended (prevents tree test corruption)
Tree test: confirms users can find specific content in the proposed hierarchy (validates findability)

rule.statement: Never treat agent-generated IA as final without tree testing — the most common IA failure mode.
rule.rationale: Agents generate internally consistent structures that may not match user mental models. Tree testing with real users is the only way to validate that users can navigate the structure, not just that the structure is logically coherent.

example.title: Organization scheme: task-based
example.note: Shop, Learn, Get Help. Shop → By Category, By Brand, Sale Items. Learn → Buying Guides, Style Tips, Product Reviews. Get Help → Order Status, Returns, Contact Us. Labels use user verbs, not company nouns. Maximum 2 levels deep.

example.title: Organization scheme: user journey
example.note: Discover, Build, Grow. Discover → Features, Solutions, Pricing. Build → Documentation, API Reference, Tutorials. Grow → Case Studies, Best Practices, Community. L1 names reflect user intent stages, not product feature categories.

antipattern.description: Navigation structure mirrors org chart (Marketing, Product, Engineering, Support as L1 categories)
antipattern.reason: Users do not know or care about internal org structure. They navigate by their goals. Org-based IA forces users to guess which department owns the content they need.

antipattern.description: Category named "Resources" or "Miscellaneous" without specific scope
antipattern.reason: "Resources" signals nothing about what it contains. Users cannot predict its contents and skip it in navigation. Everything needs a specific, content-accurate label.

antipattern.description: IA change that affects URL structure shipped without SEO review
antipattern.reason: URL structure changes break search engine indexing and user-bookmarked links. Any IA change touching URLs requires human sign-off and redirect planning before deploy.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Agent receives product spec or feature list. Produces: (1) proposed category structure at L1 and L2, (2) taxonomy document defining content types and tag groups, (3) sitemap skeleton in markdown, (4) validation plan specifying card sort type and tree test scope. Second agent pass takes card sort results and tree test failure data and produces revised sitemap with changes justified by data.

section.name: where-ia-fails
section.p: Agents generating IA from a feature list will produce an internally consistent structure that may not match user mental models — always validate with card sorting or tree testing before building. Taxonomy decisions involve business, legal, and content governance constraints that are invisible to the agent without explicit input. Navigation depth vs. breadth trade-offs (2 levels wide vs. 4 levels deep) require user task data the agent rarely has access to; it will default to shallow and broad, which is usually right but not always. Large-scale IA (thousands of content items) requires faceted taxonomy and search architecture that goes well beyond what a markdown sitemap can represent. Agents will not proactively identify "orphan" content (pages with no logical parent) without a complete content inventory as input.

section.name: agentic-workflow-detailed
section.p: A Claude agent receives a product spec or feature list and produces: (1) a proposed category structure at L1 and L2, (2) a taxonomy document defining content types, attributes, and tag groups, (3) a sitemap skeleton in markdown, and (4) a validation plan recommending which card sort type and tree test scope to run. A second agent pass takes card sort results and tree test failure data and produces a revised sitemap with specific changes justified by the data.

antipattern.description: Agents generating sitemaps from feature lists will create IA that mirrors the feature roadmap, not the user's mental model
antipattern.reason: Explicitly instruct to use user goal framing in prompts

antipattern.description: Navigation label generation tends toward corporate-speak ("Solutions", "Resources", "Platform")
antipattern.reason: Require plain-language alternatives in the prompt

antipattern.description: Agents do not spontaneously account for SEO implications of URL/category structure
antipattern.reason: If SEO matters, add a constraint to the prompt ("categories should be primary keywords")

antipattern.description: Human sign-off required before any IA change that affects URL structure
antipattern.reason: Broken URLs cause SEO damage and user-bookmarked links to fail

antipattern.description: Tree test validation is non-optional; treating agent-generated IA as final without testing
antipattern.reason: The most common IA failure mode

list.item: Start with user tasks, not content types — IA should be organized around what users want to do, not how your team organizes work internally
4-7 L1 categories is the sweet spot for top-level navigation; below 4 feels sparse, above 7 exceeds comfortable scanning range
Validate labels with 5 users before committing to a tree test — a wrong label makes tree test data uninterpretable
Design for growth: define rules for where new content types will go before launching, not after content starts accumulating
Document the IA in version-controlled markdown (not only in Figma) so engineering, SEO, and content teams can reference and diff it

template.purpose: Strategy document: research summary, organization approach, navigation design, labeling, search, validation plan
Sitemap skeleton with page inventory table
Taxonomy document: content types, categories, tags, naming conventions
